perturbation.original_text: Company shall promote and advertise the Products generally, at its own expense, and shall furnish Distributor copies at all advertisement and promotional materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall endeavor to promote and advertise the Products generally, and may furnish Distributor copies of some advertisement and promotional materials, as reasonably practical.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a clear obligation for the company to promote, advertise, and furnish copies of materials. The modified text weakens this by using 'endeavor' and 'may,' making the obligation discretionary and ambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to promote and advertise the products, it becomes easier for the Company to engage in deceptive practices related to the promotion of the Products without directly violating the contract, potentially misleading consumers about the value or marketability of the products violating 815 ILCS 505/2

perturbation.justification: The original text clearly mandates the company to promote, advertise, and provide copies of materials. The changed text uses 'endeavor' and 'may,' making the obligation discretionary, falling under the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition. The law explanation suggests that this weakening could facilitate deceptive practices violating 815 ILCS 505/2, even though the provided snippet does not specify this law. The modification introduces ambiguity into a previously clear obligation, potentially leading to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Company warrants that the Products will be designed, manufactured, constructed, assembled and packaged in a workmanlike manner and that such Products shall be fully functional and fit for their intended purposes. Company further warrants that the Products sold hereunder shall be free from defects in design, materials and workmanship for a period of twenty-four (24) months after delivery to Distributor's end-user.
perturbation.changed_text: Company generally warrants that the Products will be designed, manufactured, constructed, assembled, and packaged acceptably and that such Products should generally be functional and suitable for their basic purpose. Company further warrants that the Products sold hereunder should usually be free from significant defects in design, materials, and workmanship for a period that may reach twenty-four (24) months after delivery to Distributor's end-user.
perturbation.explanation: The original text gives a clear warranty for the products. The modified version uses vague terms like 'generally', 'acceptably', 'should', and 'may reach', significantly weakening the warranty and making its enforcement difficult due to the subjective nature of these terms. The term 'basic purpose' also reduces the protection that consumers or distributors might expect.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Uniform Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's vague language regarding the functionality and defect-free nature of the products undermines the implied warranty of merchantability under the Illinois UCC (810 ILCS 5/2-314). The UCC requires that goods be 'merchantable,' meaning they are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. The changes weaken the Company's guarantee and conflict with the expected standards of product quality.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 815 ILCS 515/ Home Repair Fraud Act. BUSINESS TRANSACTIONS (815 ILCS 515/) Home Repair Fraud Act. 815 ILCS 515/1 (815 ILCS 515/1) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 1601) Sec. 1. This Act shall be known and may be cited as the "Home Repair Fraud Act". (Source: P.A. 84-1270.) 815 ILCS 515/2 (815 ILCS 515/2) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 1602) Sec. 2. Definitions. As used in this Act, unless the context otherwise requires: (a) "Home Repair" means the fixing, replacing, altering, converting, modernizing, improving of or the making of an addition to any real property primarily designed or used as a residence. (1) Home repair shall include the construction, installation, replacement or improvement of driveways, swimming pools, porches, kitchens, chimneys, chimney liners, garages, fences, fallout shelters, central air conditioning, central heating, boilers, furnaces, hot water heaters, electrical wiring, sewers, plumbing fixtures, storm doors, storm windows, awnings and other improvements to structures within the residence or upon the land adjacent thereto. (2) Home repair shall not include the sale, installation, cleaning or repair of carpets; the sale of goods or materials by a merchant who does not directly or through a subsidiary perform any work or labor in connection with the installation or application of the goods or materials; the repair, installation, replacement or connection of any home appliance including but not limited to disposals, refrigerators, ranges, garage door openers, television antennas, washing machines, telephones or other home appliances when the person replacing, installing, repairing or connecting such home appliance are employees or agents of the merchant that sold the home appliance; or landscaping. (b) "Person" means any individual, partnership, corporation, business, trust or other legal entity. (c) "Residence" means a single or multiple family dwelling, including but not limited to a single family home, apartment building, condominium, duplex or townhouse which is used or intended to be used by its occupants as their dwelling place. Nothing in this Act shall be construed to apply to original constru

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that directly contradict the implied warranty of merchantability as it relates to product quality and functionality. The original text provides a clear warranty, while the changed text uses vague terms like 'generally', 'acceptably', and 'should,' which weaken the guarantee. The Law Explanation specifically mentions that this undermines the implied warranty of merchantability under the Illinois UCC (810 ILCS 5/2-314), requiring goods to be fit for their ordinary purposes. Although snippet 2 mentions the Illinois Commercial Code, it is too vague to use as support. The perturbation explanation states that the changes make the enforcement of the warranty difficult. Therefore, the introduction of ambiguity weakens legally binding assurances, constituting a contradiction.
